response: Architects often choose interfaces over abstract classes to decouple components, preventing a rigid file structure that could break if new relationships emerge. This approach mitigates the lack of foresight regarding every possible future path an application might take. Unlike inheritance, which forces a strict hierarchy, interfaces allow unrelated objects to share behavior without implying they are part of the same lineage. You can mix and match these contracts like ingredients in a salad, ensuring flexibility even when the full scope of object interactions is unknown at design time.
